source: Hand-curated queue of legends, moments, umpires, broadcasters, executives, and historical events for future addition to the Cardinals tab, History tab, or Stories dataset. Does NOT duplicate entries already in cardinals-links.json, historical-videos.json, stories.json, or on-this-day-seed.json.
note: The weekly-batch GitHub Action (.github/workflows/weekly-batch.yml) pulls the next N entries with status='pending' each Monday and opens a review Issue. When an entry is approved and added to a main file, set status='active' and note the destination. Rejected entries become status='rejected'.
priorityNote: 1=highest priority (featured material, iconic figures), 2=solid addition, 3=fill-in / deep-cut. Week selector favors lower numbers first, ties broken by id alphabetical for reproducibility.

entries.name: Mark McGwire
entries.summary: 1998 NL home run record chase — 70 HRs, broke Maris's 37-year record with #62 off Steve Trachsel at Busch Stadium on Sep 8.

entries.name: Willie McGee
entries.summary: 1985 NL MVP. Two-time batting champion. Beloved Cardinals outfielder across three tours with the team.

entries.name: Jim Edmonds
entries.summary: Eight Gold Gloves in center field. Iconic diving catches. Key bat on 2004 pennant and 2006 World Series champion.

entries.name: Chris Carpenter
entries.summary: 2005 NL Cy Young. Ace of the 2006 and 2011 World Series champions; threw the decisive Game 5 of the 2011 NLDS on three days rest.

entries.name: Adam Wainwright
entries.summary: 18-year Cardinal (2005-2023). Threw the curveball that struck out Carlos Beltran to end the 2006 NLCS. 200 career wins. Molina's longtime battery partner.

entries.name: Jack Buck
entries.summary: Voice of the Cardinals for 47 years. Coined 'Go crazy, folks, go crazy!' for Ozzie Smith's 1985 NLCS walk-off. Ford C. Frick Award 1987.

entries.name: Ken Boyer
entries.summary: 1964 NL MVP and 1964 World Series champion. Retired #14. Captain of the Cardinals during their return to glory.

entries.name: Curt Flood
entries.summary: Challenged MLB's reserve clause in 1969 after being traded; his lawsuit (Flood v. Kuhn) paved the way for free agency. Seven Gold Gloves in CF for the Cardinals.

entries.name: David Freese
entries.summary: 2011 NLCS and World Series MVP. Hit the walk-off HR in Game 6 of the 2011 World Series — twice rallying the Cardinals from their final strike.

entries.name: Keith Hernandez
entries.summary: 1979 NL co-MVP. Eleven straight Gold Gloves at first base (several with STL). Key piece of the 1982 World Series champion.

entries.name: Tommy Herr
entries.summary: Second baseman on the 1982 WS champion and 1985 and 1987 pennant winners. 110 RBIs in 1985 — remarkable for a non-power hitter (only 8 HRs).

entries.name: Bob Forsch
entries.summary: Two no-hitters for the Cardinals (1978, 1983). 15-year Cardinal. Brother of Phillies/Astros pitcher Ken Forsch.

entries.name: Rick Ankiel
entries.summary: Rare pitcher-to-outfielder conversion. Top pitching prospect whose control vanished in the 2000 NLDS; returned to MLB years later as a power-hitting OF.

entries.name: John Tudor
entries.summary: 1985 season for the ages: 21-8 with 10 shutouts and 1.93 ERA — finished second to Doc Gooden in Cy Young voting.

entries.name: Ray Lankford
entries.summary: One of the best all-around Cardinals outfielders of the 1990s. Ranks high on franchise leaderboards in HRs, extra-base hits, and stolen bases.

entries.name: Matt Holliday
entries.summary: 2009 trade acquisition who signed long-term. Batted in the middle of the order through the 2011 World Series title run.

entries.name: Paul Goldschmidt
entries.summary: 2022 NL MVP. Seven-time All-Star first baseman. Anchored the Cardinals' lineup since the 2019 trade from Arizona.

entries.name: Nolan Arenado
entries.summary: Ten Gold Gloves at third base and counting. Acquired from Colorado in 2021. 10-time All-Star.

entries.name: Todd Worrell
entries.summary: 1986 NL Rookie of the Year. Closer for the 1985 and 1987 pennant winners. Three-time All-Star.

entries.name: Lee Smith
entries.summary: Dominant closer during his 1990-93 Cardinals run. Hall of Fame 2019 (Veterans Committee). 478 career saves.

entries.name: Orlando Cepeda
entries.summary: 1967 NL MVP with the pennant-winning Cardinals — first unanimous selection in NL history at that point. Hall of Fame 1999.

entries.name: Scott Rolen
entries.summary: Third baseman on the 2006 World Series champion. Eight Gold Gloves overall. Hall of Fame 2023.

entries.name: Pepper Martin
entries.summary: Gas House Gang leadoff man and face of the 1934 champions. Stole home in the 1931 World Series. Three-time All-Star.

entries.name: Joe Medwick
entries.summary: 1937 NL Triple Crown and MVP — the last NL Triple Crown winner until 2012. Ejected from Game 7 of the 1934 WS for his own safety after Tigers fans threw fruit at him. HOF 1968.

entries.name: Vince Coleman
entries.summary: Led the NL in stolen bases his first six seasons (1985-1990). 752 career steals. Infamous 1985 tarp-accident injury during the NLCS.

entries.name: Edgar Renteria
entries.summary: Shortstop on the 2004 pennant winner and 2006 WS champion. Two-time Gold Glove with the Cardinals.

entries.name: Matt Carpenter
entries.summary: Three-time All-Star during his Cardinals prime (2013-2019). Led the NL in runs, hits, and doubles in 2013.

entries.name: Jason Isringhausen
entries.summary: 300 career saves, most in Cardinals franchise history at the time of his departure. Anchor of the 2006 WS champion bullpen.

entries.name: Lance Berkman
entries.summary: 2011 World Series MVP runner-up. Tied David Freese's Game 6 rally with a two-out, two-strike single in the 10th.

entries.name: Mike Shannon
entries.summary: Played for the 1964 and 1967 WS champions as a third baseman/OF, then 50+ years broadcasting the Cardinals. Ford C. Frick Award 2021.

entries.name: Harry Caray
entries.summary: Voice of the Cardinals 1945-1969 before his later Cubs tenure. Ford C. Frick Award 1989.

entries.name: George Hendrick
entries.summary: Known as Silent George. Drove in the run that clinched the 1982 World Series. Four-time All-Star.

entries.name: Andy Van Slyke
entries.summary: Standout defensive outfielder on the 1982 WS champion. Later starred for Pittsburgh after the Tony Pena trade.

entries.name: Bake McBride
entries.summary: 1974 NL Rookie of the Year. Played on the 1974 Cardinals who finished 2nd in the NL East with 86 wins.

entries.name: Reggie Smith
entries.summary: Switch-hitting OF. Two seasons with the Cardinals (1974-1975) between his Red Sox and Dodgers years. Seven-time All-Star.

entries.name: Gene Tenace
entries.summary: 1972 World Series MVP with Oakland. Spent 1981-1982 with the Cardinals, winning a second ring in 1982.

entries.name: Joaquín Andújar
entries.summary: Three-time All-Star pitcher. Ace of the 1982 and 1985 Cardinals. Won Games 3 and 7 of the 1982 World Series.

entries.name: 1926 WS Game 7 — Alexander strikes out Lazzeri
entries.summary: Grover Cleveland Alexander, pulled from the rotation for relief duty, fanned Tony Lazzeri with the bases loaded in the 7th inning of Game 7 to preserve the Cardinals' first title.

entries.name: 1944 Streetcar Series
entries.summary: The Cardinals beat the St. Louis Browns in the only all-St. Louis World Series, played entirely at Sportsman's Park during WWII.

entries.name: 1982 WS — Sutter's final out
entries.summary: Bruce Sutter froze Gorman Thomas with a split-finger fastball for the final out of the 1982 World Series, giving the Cardinals their first title in 15 years.

entries.name: 1985 WS Game 6 — The Denkinger Call
entries.summary: Umpire Don Denkinger called Jorge Orta safe at first in the 9th inning of Game 6 with the Cardinals one out from the title. The Royals rallied, won Game 6, and took Game 7.

entries.name: 2006 WS — Eckstein MVP
entries.summary: 5'7" shortstop David Eckstein won World Series MVP, going 8-for-22 with three doubles as the Cardinals beat Detroit in 5.

entries.name: 2011 NLDS — The Rally Squirrel
entries.summary: During Game 4 of the 2011 NLDS at Busch Stadium, a squirrel ran across home plate during a pitch. It returned for Game 5 and became the unofficial mascot of the championship run.

entries.name: 2011 WS Game 6 — Freese walk-off
entries.summary: Down to their final strike twice, the Cardinals rallied against Texas. David Freese tripled to tie it in the 9th, then walked it off with a HR in the 11th. Game 7 won the next night.

entries.name: Ozzie Smith's 1985 NLCS walk-off
entries.summary: Smith's first career left-handed HR won Game 5 of the 1985 NLCS vs. the Dodgers, prompting Jack Buck's famous 'Go crazy, folks!' call.

entries.name: Babe Ruth
entries.summary: 714 career HR. Seven-time World Series champion (3 with Red Sox as a pitcher, 4 with Yankees). First-ever Hall of Fame class, 1936. Arguably the most consequential figure in baseball history.

entries.name: Ty Cobb
entries.summary: Career .366 batting average — highest in MLB history. 4,189 hits (2nd all-time). First player elected to the Hall of Fame (1936, with Ruth).

entries.name: Honus Wagner
entries.summary: Eight-time NL batting champion, all with Pittsburgh. First-ballot Hall of Famer (1936). His T206 tobacco card is the most valuable baseball card in existence.

entries.name: Christy Mathewson
entries.summary: 373 career wins (T-3rd all-time). Threw three shutouts in the 1905 World Series. WWI veteran — damaged lungs from accidental mustard gas exposure hastened his death at 45. HOF 1936.

entries.name: Walter Johnson
entries.summary: 417 career wins (2nd all-time). 110 career shutouts — still the MLB record. 21 seasons with the Washington Senators. HOF 1936.

entries.name: Lou Gehrig
entries.summary: 'The Iron Horse.' 2,130 consecutive games. Seven-time WS champion with the Yankees. ALS forced retirement in 1939 — his 'luckiest man' speech is one of the most famous in sports history.

entries.name: Joe DiMaggio
entries.summary: 56-game hitting streak in 1941 — still the MLB record. Three-time MVP. Nine-time WS champion. WWII veteran.

entries.name: Ted Williams
entries.summary: Last player to hit .400 (.406 in 1941). Two-time MVP. Two Triple Crowns. Marine pilot in WWII and Korea (missed ~5 seasons total).

entries.name: Mickey Mantle
entries.summary: Three-time AL MVP. 1956 Triple Crown. Seven-time WS champion. 18 career WS home runs is still the record.

entries.name: Yogi Berra
entries.summary: Three-time AL MVP. 10 World Series rings as a player — most in MLB history. Later managed the Yankees and Mets to pennants. D-Day veteran.

entries.name: Sandy Koufax
entries.summary: Four no-hitters including a perfect game (1965). Three Cy Youngs (first three ever). Retired at 30 due to elbow arthritis. HOF 1972 at 36 — youngest ever.

entries.name: Roberto Clemente
entries.summary: First Latin American Hall of Famer (1973 — five-year wait waived after his death delivering earthquake aid to Nicaragua). 1966 NL MVP. 3,000 career hits.

entries.name: Frank Robinson
entries.summary: Only player to win MVP in both leagues (NL 1961 Cincinnati, AL 1966 Baltimore Triple Crown). First Black MLB manager (1975, Cleveland).

entries.name: Willie McCovey
entries.summary: 1969 NL MVP. 521 career HR. Six-time All-Star first baseman for the Giants. HOF 1986.

entries.name: Nolan Ryan
entries.summary: 5,714 career strikeouts — still the MLB record by nearly 850. Seven no-hitters (also a record). 27-year career across four teams.

entries.name: Tom Seaver
entries.summary: Three-time Cy Young. 311 career wins. Led the 1969 Miracle Mets to the World Series title. HOF 1992 with 98.8% of the vote (a record at the time).

entries.name: Roy Campanella
entries.summary: Three-time NL MVP Brooklyn Dodgers catcher. Career cut short by a 1958 car accident that paralyzed him. HOF 1969.

entries.name: Don Drysdale
entries.summary: 1962 Cy Young. Set the MLB consecutive-scoreless-innings record at 58 2/3 in 1968 (broken by Hershiser in 1988).

entries.name: Reggie Jackson
entries.summary: 'Mr. October.' Three HRs on three pitches in Game 6 of the 1977 World Series. Five WS rings across three teams. HOF 1993.

entries.name: George Brett
entries.summary: 1980 AL MVP (.390 batting average that season). 13-time All-Star. Pine Tar Incident of 1983 became one of the most famous umpire disputes ever.

entries.name: Tony Gwynn
entries.summary: Eight NL batting titles. Career .338 average. 3,141 career hits — all with the Padres across 20 seasons. HOF 2007.

entries.name: Rickey Henderson
entries.summary: 1,406 career stolen bases — most in MLB history by nearly 500. 2,295 runs scored, also the all-time record. 1990 AL MVP.

entries.name: Ken Griffey Jr.
entries.summary: 1997 AL MVP. 630 career HRs. 10 Gold Gloves in center field. Hall of Fame 2016 with 99.3% — a record at the time.

entries.name: Derek Jeter
entries.summary: Captain of five Yankees championship teams. 3,465 career hits (6th all-time). Hall of Fame 2020 (unanimous but one voter).

entries.name: Randy Johnson
entries.summary: Five Cy Youngs. Perfect game in 2004 at age 40 — oldest ever. 303 career wins. Co-MVP of the 2001 World Series.

entries.name: Pedro Martínez
entries.summary: Three Cy Youngs. Historic 1999-2000 Boston seasons (ERA+ of 291 and 243 — among the best two-year runs ever). HOF 2015.

entries.name: Greg Maddux
entries.summary: Four consecutive Cy Youngs (1992-95). 355 career wins. 18 Gold Gloves. Master of movement and command.

entries.name: David Ortiz
entries.summary: 'Big Papi.' Three-time World Series champion with the Red Sox. Key figure in ending the 86-year curse (2004). 541 career HRs.

entries.name: Chipper Jones
entries.summary: 1999 NL MVP. 19-year Brave. Switch-hitter with a career .303 average. 2018 first-ballot Hall of Famer.

entries.name: Ichiro Suzuki
entries.summary: 2001 AL MVP and Rookie of the Year. 257 hits in 2004 — single-season MLB record. 4,367 combined hits (Japan + MLB).

entries.name: Pete Rose
entries.summary: 4,256 career hits — the all-time record. 1973 NL MVP. Banned from baseball in 1989 for gambling on games as a manager.

entries.name: Johnny Bench
entries.summary: Two-time NL MVP. 10 Gold Gloves behind the plate. Widely considered the greatest catcher of all time. Big Red Machine's backbone.

entries.name: Joe Morgan
entries.summary: Back-to-back NL MVPs 1975-76 with the Big Red Machine. Five Gold Gloves at second base. Longtime ESPN broadcaster.

entries.name: Carl Yastrzemski
entries.summary: 1967 Triple Crown and AL MVP. Last player to win a Triple Crown until 2012. 23-year Red Sox.

entries.name: Whitey Ford
entries.summary: 'The Chairman of the Board.' Six-time WS champion Yankees ace. 1961 Cy Young. Career 2.75 ERA.

entries.name: Rollie Fingers
entries.summary: 1981 AL Cy Young and MVP. Iconic handlebar mustache. First reliever to reach 300 saves. Three WS rings with Oakland.

entries.name: Dennis Eckersley
entries.summary: Starter-turned-closer transition defined the modern role. 1992 AL Cy Young and MVP as a reliever. 390 career saves.

entries.name: Trevor Hoffman
entries.summary: 601 career saves. His 'Hells Bells' entrance in San Diego remains one of the most memorable in MLB. HOF 2018.

entries.name: Craig Biggio
entries.summary: 3,060 career hits — all with the Astros. Played catcher, second base, and outfield at All-Star level. HOF 2015.

entries.name: Jeff Bagwell
entries.summary: 1994 NL MVP. Long-tenured Astro first baseman. Retired 3rd all-time among Astros in games played. HOF 2017.

entries.name: Kirby Puckett
entries.summary: Minnesota Twin for his entire 12-year career. Clutch HR in Game 6 of the 1991 World Series. Career ended abruptly by glaucoma. HOF 2001.

entries.name: Eddie Murray
entries.summary: Switch-hitting first baseman. One of only six players with 3,000 hits and 500 HRs. HOF 2003.

entries.name: Josh Gibson
entries.summary: Widely considered the greatest catcher of all time. Negro Leagues career batting average reported above .370. MLB officially recognized his NL stats in 2020. Died at 35 in 1947, months before Jackie Robinson's debut.

entries.name: Oscar Charleston
entries.summary: Widely considered the greatest all-around Negro Leagues player. Bill James ranked him among the 10 best players ever. HOF 1976.

entries.name: Cool Papa Bell
entries.summary: Famed for his speed — Satchel Paige said he was 'so fast he could turn off the light and be in bed before the room got dark.' HOF 1974.

entries.name: Buck O'Neil
entries.summary: Kansas City Monarchs first baseman. First Black coach in MLB (Cubs, 1962). Tireless Negro Leagues historian and ambassador. Posthumous HOF 2022.

entries.name: Rube Foster
entries.summary: 'The Father of Black Baseball.' Founded the first successful Negro National League in 1920. HOF 1981.

entries.name: Smokey Joe Williams
entries.summary: Pre-Negro Leagues pitcher (1910s-20s) who reportedly beat barnstorming Ty Cobb-led teams. HOF 1999.

entries.name: Pop Lloyd
entries.summary: Shortstop compared favorably to Honus Wagner in his prime. Deadball era Negro Leagues great. HOF 1977.

entries.name: Martín Dihigo
entries.summary: Cuban-born two-way star who played every position. Member of Cuban, Mexican, Dominican, Venezuelan, and American halls of fame. MLB HOF 1977.

entries.name: Bill Klem
entries.summary: 'The Old Arbitrator.' 36-year NL umpire. Invented the umpire's arm signals that are still used today. HOF 1953.

entries.name: Nestor Chylak
entries.summary: AL umpire 1954-1978. WWII Bronze Star and Purple Heart at the Battle of the Bulge. HOF 1999.

entries.name: Al Barlick
entries.summary: 28-year NL umpire. Worked seven World Series. HOF 1989. Known for his booming strike call.

entries.name: Vin Scully
entries.summary: Voice of the Dodgers 1950-2016 — 67 seasons. Called Hank Aaron's 715th, Koufax's perfect game, Gibson's 1988 walk-off. Ford C. Frick Award 1982.

entries.name: Ernie Harwell
entries.summary: Voice of the Detroit Tigers for 42 years. Known for his poetic phrasing. Ford C. Frick Award 1981.

entries.name: Bob Uecker
entries.summary: 'Mr. Baseball.' Brewers broadcaster since 1971. Brief playing career, including with the 1964 Cardinals WS champions. Ford C. Frick Award 2003.

entries.name: Mel Allen
entries.summary: 'The Voice of the Yankees' from 1940s-60s. First Ford C. Frick Award recipient (1978, tied with Red Barber).

entries.name: Red Barber
entries.summary: Brooklyn Dodgers radio announcer 1939-1953. Called Jackie Robinson's debut. Known for Southern phrasing like 'sittin' in the catbird seat.'

entries.name: Jon Miller
entries.summary: Giants lead broadcaster since 1997. ESPN Sunday Night Baseball voice 1990-2010. Ford C. Frick Award 2010.

entries.name: Phil Rizzuto
entries.summary: Yankees shortstop (1941-56, missed WWII) turned broadcaster (1957-1996). 'Holy cow!' catchphrase. Ford C. Frick 1994.

entries.name: Connie Mack
entries.summary: Managed the Philadelphia Athletics for 50 seasons (1901-1950). 3,731 career wins — the all-time record, likely never to be broken.

entries.name: Casey Stengel
entries.summary: Managed seven Yankees World Series champions (1949-1960). Also led the expansion 1962 Mets. Famously quotable. HOF 1966.

entries.name: Sparky Anderson
entries.summary: First manager to win World Series in both leagues (Reds 1975-76, Tigers 1984). Big Red Machine architect. HOF 2000.

entries.name: Earl Weaver
entries.summary: Baltimore manager 1968-82, 1985-86. Famous for his belief in 'pitching, defense, and the three-run homer.' Six division titles. HOF 1996.

entries.name: Tommy Lasorda
entries.summary: Dodgers manager 1976-96. Two World Series titles (1981, 1988). 'I bleed Dodger blue.' HOF 1997.

entries.name: Bobby Cox
entries.summary: Managed the Braves' 14 consecutive division titles run (1991-2005). 1995 World Series champion. HOF 2014.

entries.name: Joe Torre
entries.summary: Four Yankees World Series titles as manager (1996, 1998-2000). 2,326 career wins. 1971 NL MVP as a player. HOF 2014.

entries.name: Billy Martin
entries.summary: Five stints as Yankees manager (1975-79, 1983, 1985, 1988). Fiery personality. 1977 World Series champion.

entries.name: Dick Williams
entries.summary: Managed the Impossible Dream 1967 Red Sox, the Swingin' A's 1972-73 titles, and the 1984 NL champion Padres. HOF 2008.

entries.name: Davey Johnson
entries.summary: Managed the 1986 Mets to a World Series title. Also led the Nationals to the 2012 NL East title.

entries.name: Branch Rickey
entries.summary: Beyond signing Jackie Robinson: invented the modern farm system in St. Louis in the 1920s. Signed the first Venezuelan and Puerto Rican stars. HOF 1967.

entries.name: Bill Veeck
entries.summary: Innovative owner (Indians, Browns, White Sox). Signed Larry Doby in 1947. Sent 3'7" Eddie Gaedel to the plate in 1951. Invented the exploding scoreboard. HOF 1991.

entries.name: Marvin Miller
entries.summary: MLBPA Executive Director 1966-83. Won free agency and ended the reserve clause. Transformed player rights more than any non-player in baseball history. HOF 2020.

entries.name: Bart Giamatti
entries.summary: Yale president turned MLB commissioner (1988-89). Banned Pete Rose for life. Died of a heart attack just days after the ban, age 51.

entries.name: Bowie Kuhn
entries.summary: MLB commissioner 1969-84. Oversaw the beginning of free agency and expansion. Suspended George Steinbrenner. HOF 2008.

entries.name: Ban Johnson
entries.summary: Founded the American League in 1901 as a major league. First AL president. Shaped early 20th-century baseball. HOF 1937.

entries.name: Kenesaw Mountain Landis
entries.summary: First MLB commissioner (1920-44), appointed after the Black Sox scandal. Banned the eight White Sox players for life. HOF 1944.

entries.name: Buddy Lewis
entries.summary: Two-time All-Star Senators outfielder. Flew 368 combat missions in the China-Burma-India theater as a C-47 pilot. Distinguished Flying Cross.

entries.name: Morrie Martin
entries.summary: WWII infantryman. Shot twice, captured by the Germans, escaped, recaptured — shot at again during a Death March. Pitched 10 MLB seasons post-war.

entries.name: Stan Musial — Navy service
entries.summary: Served in the Navy 1945 as a ship repairman in Hawaii, missing the 1945 season. Returned to win three MVPs in the next five years.

entries.name: Phil Rizzuto — Navy Pacific
entries.summary: Lost three seasons (1943-45) in the Navy. Caught malaria in the Pacific. Returned to win 1950 AL MVP.

entries.name: Ralph Branca — Army service
entries.summary: Served in the Army between 1951 and 1952 seasons. Four years after giving up the Shot Heard 'Round the World, his military service bookended the most famous pitch of his life.

entries.name: Billy Southworth Jr.
entries.summary: Son of Cardinals manager Billy Southworth. Minor leaguer who enlisted after Pearl Harbor. Killed February 15, 1945 when his B-29 crashed in Flushing Bay during a training flight.

entries.name: The Shot Heard 'Round the World (Thomson 1951)
entries.summary: Bobby Thomson's 3-run HR off Ralph Branca at the Polo Grounds won the NL pennant playoff for the Giants. Russ Hodges: 'The Giants win the pennant! The Giants win the pennant!'

entries.name: Bill Mazeroski 1960 WS walk-off
entries.summary: Only walk-off HR in a World Series Game 7. Pirates over Yankees 10-9 to end the 1960 series. 9th inning, bottom of the inning, off Ralph Terry.

entries.name: Don Larsen's perfect game 1956 WS
entries.summary: Only perfect game in World Series history. Yankees' Larsen retired all 27 Brooklyn Dodgers in Game 5. Yogi Berra's leap into his arms is the iconic image.

entries.name: 1919 Black Sox scandal
entries.summary: Eight Chicago White Sox players banned for life for throwing the 1919 World Series to the Reds. Led to the creation of the MLB commissioner role.

entries.name: The Pine Tar Incident (Brett 1983)
entries.summary: George Brett's HR off Goose Gossage was initially disallowed because pine tar on his bat extended beyond the legal limit. Brett's eruption from the dugout became iconic. AL president Lee MacPhail overturned the call; game resumed weeks later.

entries.name: 1986 WS Game 6 — Buckner's error
entries.summary: Red Sox one out from ending the 68-year title drought; Mookie Wilson's grounder went through Bill Buckner's legs, scoring Ray Knight for a Mets win. Boston lost Game 7. The curse continued until 2004.

entries.name: 1927 Yankees Murderers' Row
entries.summary: 110-44 record, widely considered the greatest team ever assembled. Ruth hit 60 HRs; Gehrig drove in 175. Swept the Pirates in the World Series.

entries.name: 2004 Red Sox ALCS comeback
entries.summary: Down 0-3 to the Yankees, Boston won four straight — the only 0-3 comeback in MLB postseason history — and went on to win the World Series, ending the 86-year curse.

entries.name: Harvey Haddix 12 perfect innings
entries.summary: May 26, 1959: Pirates' Haddix retired 36 straight Milwaukee Braves before an error in the 13th. Lost the game 1-0. Not officially a perfect game but one of the greatest pitching performances ever.

entries.name: Kerry Wood's 20-strikeout game
entries.summary: May 6, 1998: 20-year-old Cubs rookie struck out 20 Astros in 9 innings — tying the MLB record. He allowed one infield hit in a dominating performance.

entries.name: Jim Abbott's no-hitter
entries.summary: September 4, 1993: Yankees' Abbott — born without a right hand — no-hit the Indians 4-0. One of the most inspirational pitching performances in MLB history.

entries.name: Randy Johnson's 2004 perfect game
entries.summary: May 18, 2004: Diamondbacks' Johnson retired all 27 Braves at age 40 — the oldest ever to throw a perfect game.

entries.name: Ichiro 257-hit season (2004)
entries.summary: Ichiro Suzuki's 2004 single-season MLB hit record. Broke George Sisler's 84-year-old mark of 257. Finished 7th in AL MVP voting.

entries.name: 2002 Angels World Series
entries.summary: Wild card Angels came back from 5-0 down in Game 6 to beat the Giants. Salmon, Spiezio, Erstad, Glaus — the Rally Monkey era's championship.

entries.name: 2000 Subway Series
entries.summary: First Yankees vs Mets World Series since the subway-era NY teams. Yankees won in 5. Iconic Game 2 moment: Clemens throwing the broken bat at Piazza.

entries.name: Big Red Machine 1975-76
entries.summary: Back-to-back World Series titles for Cincinnati. Rose, Morgan, Bench, Perez, Foster, Griffey Sr., Concepcion. One of the greatest teams ever assembled.

entries.name: 1984 Tigers start 35-5
entries.summary: The Tigers started 35-5 and never looked back en route to a 104-win season and a World Series win over the Padres.

entries.name: 1993 Phillies and Carter HR
entries.summary: Joe Carter's walk-off 3-run HR off Mitch Williams in Game 6 clinched back-to-back titles for Toronto and ended Philadelphia's upstart run.

entries.name: 1977-78 Yankees dynasty
entries.summary: Back-to-back World Series over the Dodgers. 'The Bronx Zoo' era: Reggie, Munson, Billy Martin, George Steinbrenner, chaos and championships.

entries.name: 1968 — The Year of the Pitcher
entries.summary: Gibson 1.12 ERA, McLain 31 wins, Yaz batted title at .301 (lowest ever). Mound was lowered and strike zone shrunk the following year in response.

entries.name: Ron Guidry's 1978
entries.summary: 25-3, 1.74 ERA, 9 shutouts, 248 K. One of the greatest single-season pitching performances ever. AL Cy Young. Yankees won the World Series.

entries.name: Doc Gooden's 1984-85
entries.summary: 1984 NL Rookie of the Year at 19. 1985 Cy Young at 20: 24-4, 1.53 ERA, 268 K. Among the most dominant consecutive seasons by a young pitcher.

entries.name: Mark 'The Bird' Fidrych 1976
entries.summary: Tigers rookie's cultural phenomenon: talking to baseballs, patting down the mound. AL Rookie of the Year. Injuries ended his career 2 years later.

entries.name: Career strikeouts leader
entries.summary: All-time strikeouts as a pitcher.
entries.question: Which pitcher holds the all-time MLB record for career strikeouts?
entries.answer: Nolan Ryan — 5,714 career strikeouts. He retired in 1993 at age 46. Randy Johnson is second with 4,875.

entries.name: Cal Ripken consecutive games
entries.summary: The streak that broke Gehrig's 2,130.
entries.question: On what date did Cal Ripken Jr. break Lou Gehrig's consecutive-games-played record, and at what number did Ripken finish?
entries.answer: September 6, 1995 (game 2,131, vs. the Angels at Camden Yards). Ripken extended the streak to 2,632 before voluntarily ending it on September 20, 1998.

entries.name: Perfect games count
entries.summary: Total perfect games in MLB history.
entries.question: Who threw the first modern (post-1900) perfect game in MLB history?
entries.answer: Cy Young — May 5, 1904, Boston Americans (later Red Sox) vs. Philadelphia Athletics. The first perfect game since 1880 (when the rules were different) and the first of the modern era.

entries.name: Most no-hitters in one season
entries.summary: A season-long record.
entries.question: Which pitcher threw the most no-hitters in a single MLB season, and how many?
entries.answer: Johnny Vander Meer (Cincinnati Reds, 1938) — back-to-back no-hitters on June 11 and June 15, 1938. The only pitcher in MLB history to throw consecutive no-hitters.

entries.name: Hits in a season
entries.summary: Single-season hits record.
entries.question: Who holds the MLB single-season hits record, and how many?
entries.answer: Ichiro Suzuki — 262 hits in 2004 (Seattle Mariners). Broke George Sisler's 1920 record of 257.

entries.name: Inaugural HOF class 1936
entries.summary: The first five.
entries.question: Who were the five players elected in the inaugural 1936 Hall of Fame class?
entries.answer: Ty Cobb, Babe Ruth, Honus Wagner, Christy Mathewson, and Walter Johnson. Cobb received the most votes (222 of 226).

entries.name: First unanimous HOF
entries.summary: The first 100% election.
entries.question: Who was the first player elected to the Hall of Fame with 100% of the vote?
entries.answer: Mariano Rivera in 2019 — 425 of 425 ballots. No player had received unanimous BBWAA election in the prior 83 years.

entries.name: Youngest HOF player
entries.summary: Earliest induction by age.
entries.question: Who is the youngest player ever inducted into the Hall of Fame?
entries.answer: Sandy Koufax — inducted in 1972 at age 36, eligible early because injury forced his retirement at 30 (the 5-year waiting period had elapsed).

entries.name: Walk-off WS Game 7 HRs
entries.summary: A rare way to end the Series.
entries.question: Which two players have hit walk-off home runs to win Game 7 of the World Series?
entries.answer: Bill Mazeroski (Pirates, 1960, vs. Yankees) and Joe Carter (Blue Jays, 1993, vs. Phillies — actually a Game 6 walk-off, not Game 7). Only Mazeroski has hit a Game 7 walk-off WS home run.

entries.name: Postseason perfect game
entries.summary: The lone postseason perfecto.
entries.question: Who threw the only perfect game in MLB postseason history?
entries.answer: Don Larsen (New York Yankees) — Game 5 of the 1956 World Series vs. the Brooklyn Dodgers, October 8, 1956. The only postseason perfect game ever thrown.

entries.name: Most WS championships franchise
entries.summary: The all-time franchise leader.
entries.question: Which franchise has won the most World Series championships, and how many?
entries.answer: New York Yankees — 27 championships (most recent in 2009). The Cardinals are second with 11.

entries.name: Slaughter's Mad Dash
entries.summary: 1946 WS Game 7.
entries.question: In Game 7 of the 1946 World Series, who scored the winning run from first base on what is now called "The Mad Dash"?
entries.answer: Enos Slaughter — Cardinals vs. Boston Red Sox at Sportsman's Park, October 15, 1946. He scored from first on a Harry Walker hit (officially scored as a double) when shortstop Johnny Pesky hesitated on the relay throw.

entries.name: Stan Musial MVPs
entries.summary: How many for "The Man".
entries.question: How many National League MVP awards did Stan Musial win, and in which years?
entries.answer: Three NL MVP awards — 1943, 1946, and 1948. He finished second in voting four other times.

entries.name: Nationals first WS title
entries.summary: The 2019 championship.
entries.question: In what year did the Washington Nationals win their first World Series, and who did they defeat?
entries.answer: 2019 — defeated the Houston Astros 4-3 in the World Series. The road team won every game (6 of 7) — the first time that had ever happened in a major North American sports championship.

entries.name: Senators relocation
entries.summary: How the Nationals got to DC.
entries.question: The Washington Nationals franchise originated as which earlier team, and in what year did they relocate to Washington?
entries.answer: The Montreal Expos (1969-2004). The franchise relocated to Washington for the 2005 season, becoming the Nationals. They were the first MLB team in DC since the Senators left in 1971.

entries.name: First MLB to enlist after Pearl Harbor
entries.summary: Cleveland's ace.
entries.question: Who was the first MLB player to enlist in the U.S. military after the attack on Pearl Harbor?
entries.answer: Bob Feller (Cleveland Indians) — enlisted in the U.S. Navy on December 8, 1941, the day after Pearl Harbor. He served four years, missing the bulk of his prime, and earned eight battle stars as a gun captain on the USS Alabama.

entries.name: First Black AL player
entries.summary: AL integration.
entries.question: Who was the first African American player in the American League, and for which team?
entries.answer: Larry Doby — debuted with the Cleveland Indians on July 5, 1947, eleven weeks after Jackie Robinson broke the NL color barrier. Hall of Fame 1998.

entries.name: Oldest active MLB park
entries.summary: Continuously used.
entries.question: Which MLB park is the oldest still in use, and in what year did it open?
entries.answer: Fenway Park (Boston Red Sox) — opened April 20, 1912. Wrigley Field (Cubs, 1914) is the second-oldest.

entries.name: Last MLB expansion
entries.summary: 30 teams since when.
entries.question: When did MLB expand to 30 teams, and which were the last two added?
entries.answer: 1998 — the Arizona Diamondbacks and Tampa Bay Devil Rays (now Rays) began play. MLB has had 30 franchises since.

entries.name: Galarraga "imperfect game"
entries.summary: A blown call on the 27th out.
entries.question: Which umpire blew the call on what would have been the 27th out of Armando Galarraga's perfect game in 2010, and how did the umpire respond afterward?
entries.answer: Jim Joyce — first-base umpire, June 2, 2010, Tigers vs. Indians. Joyce ruled Jason Donald safe; replay showed he was out. Joyce publicly apologized in tears immediately after the game and embraced Galarraga, who delivered the lineup card the next day. The play prompted MLB to expand replay review.

entries.name: Musial's 3,000th hit (1958-05-13)
entries.summary: May 13, 1958: Stan Musial pinch-hits a double off Moe Drabowsky for career hit #3,000 at Wrigley Field.

seedEntry.title: Stan Musial's 3,000th career hit
seedEntry.description: Musial pinch-hit a two-run double off the Cubs' Moe Drabowsky at Wrigley Field for hit #3,000. He became the eighth player to reach the milestone and the first National Leaguer in 16 years.

entries.name: Musial's final game (1963-09-29)
entries.summary: Sept 29, 1963: Stan Musial doubles off Jim Maloney in his final at-bat, ending a 22-year career with 3,630 hits.

seedEntry.title: Stan Musial's final game
seedEntry.description: Musial singled and doubled off Cincinnati's Jim Maloney at Sportsman's Park, finishing with 3,630 career hits — exactly half at home and half on the road. He was 42 years old. Pete Rose, in his rookie season, played second base for the Reds that day.

entries.name: Freese's Game 6 walk-off (2011-10-27)
entries.summary: Oct 27, 2011: David Freese walks off the Rangers in the 11th of WS Game 6 — Cards won Game 7 the next night.

seedEntry.title: David Freese's Game 6 World Series walk-off
seedEntry.description: Down to their last strike twice, the Cardinals tied the game on Freese's two-out triple in the 9th, then won it on Freese's solo home run off Mark Lowe in the 11th. Joe Buck called it: "We will see you tomorrow night." Freese was named World Series MVP after the Cardinals won Game 7 the next night.

entries.name: Cards win 2011 WS Game 7 (2011-10-28)
entries.summary: Oct 28, 2011: Cardinals beat Texas 6-2 in Game 7 for their 11th championship — Freese WS MVP.

seedEntry.title: Cardinals win 2011 World Series
seedEntry.description: St. Louis beat the Texas Rangers 6-2 in Game 7 at Busch Stadium for their 11th World Series title — second-most in MLB history. Chris Carpenter started on three days' rest. David Freese, who hit the Game 6 walk-off, won World Series MVP.

entries.name: Denkinger's blown call (1985-10-26)
entries.summary: Oct 26, 1985: Don Denkinger calls Jorge Orta safe at first in Game 6 of the WS; replay shows out. Royals win Series.

seedEntry.title: Don Denkinger's blown call in World Series Game 6
seedEntry.description: With the Cardinals leading 1-0 in the bottom of the 9th, Royals pinch hitter Jorge Orta hit a chopper to first; replay clearly showed Orta out, but first-base umpire Don Denkinger called him safe. The Royals scored twice to win and forced Game 7, which they took 11-0. The call became a foundational argument for instant replay in MLB and Cardinals fans never quite forgave Denkinger.

entries.name: Ted Williams MLB debut (1939-04-23)
entries.summary: April 23, 1939: Ted Williams plays his first big-league game at Yankee Stadium for the Red Sox.

seedEntry.title: Ted Williams's MLB debut
seedEntry.description: Twenty-year-old Williams went 1-for-4 with a double off Yankees ace Red Ruffing in his first major-league game. He would go on to a .344 career batting average, two MVP awards, two Triple Crowns, and the last .400 season in MLB history.

entries.name: First Black MLB manager (1975-04-08)
entries.summary: April 8, 1975: Frank Robinson manages Cleveland in their opener — the first Black manager in MLB history. He homered in his first AB.

seedEntry.title: Frank Robinson becomes first Black manager in MLB
seedEntry.description: Player-manager Frank Robinson led the Cleveland Indians out of the dugout for Opening Day against the Yankees, becoming the first Black manager in MLB history — 28 years after Jackie Robinson broke the color barrier as a player. Robinson, then 39 and still active as a hitter, homered in his first at-bat. Cleveland won 5-3.

entries.name: Negro Leagues recognized (2020-12-16)
entries.summary: Dec 16, 2020: MLB officially recognizes seven Negro Leagues (1920-1948) as Major Leagues, redefining record books.

seedEntry.title: MLB recognizes Negro Leagues as Major Leagues
seedEntry.description: MLB designated seven Negro Leagues operating between 1920 and 1948 as official Major Leagues, integrating the statistics of about 3,400 Black players (including Josh Gibson, Satchel Paige, and Oscar Charleston) into the historical record. Josh Gibson eventually became MLB's all-time batting average leader after the recognition.

entries.name: Ernie Shore's 26-up relief (1917-06-23)
entries.summary: June 23, 1917: Babe Ruth walks the leadoff batter and is ejected for arguing; Ernie Shore retires the next 26 in relief.

seedEntry.title: Ernie Shore's relief "perfect game"
seedEntry.description: Boston Red Sox starter Babe Ruth walked Washington's Ray Morgan to lead off the game, then was ejected for arguing balls and strikes. Reliever Ernie Shore came in cold; Morgan was caught stealing on Shore's first pitch, and Shore retired the next 26 batters in order — 27 outs total under his watch. Originally credited as a "perfect game," it was reclassified in 1991 as a combined no-hitter.

entries.name: Koufax perfect game (1965-09-09)
entries.summary: Sept 9, 1965: Sandy Koufax retires all 27 Cubs at Dodger Stadium, striking out 14. Vin Scully's 9th-inning call.

seedEntry.title: Sandy Koufax pitches a perfect game
seedEntry.description: Koufax retired all 27 Chicago Cubs at Dodger Stadium, striking out 14. He struck out the side in the 9th to seal his 4th career no-hitter and lone perfect game, defeating Bob Hendley 1-0. Vin Scully's call — "two and two to Harvey Kuenn, one strike away…" — is one of the most replayed broadcasts in baseball history.

entries.name: Rose breaks Cobb's hits record (1985-09-11)
entries.summary: Sept 11, 1985: Pete Rose singles off Eric Show for hit #4,192, passing Ty Cobb as MLB's all-time hits leader.

seedEntry.title: Pete Rose breaks Ty Cobb's all-time hits record
seedEntry.description: Player-manager Pete Rose lined a single to left-center off the Padres' Eric Show at Riverfront Stadium for career hit #4,192, passing Ty Cobb on the all-time list. Riverfront erupted for over seven minutes; Marge Schott embraced Rose at first base, and his son Pete Jr. ran out from the dugout. Rose finished with 4,256 career hits.

entries.name: Williams' final-AB home run (1960-09-28)
entries.summary: Sept 28, 1960: Ted Williams homers off Jack Fisher in his final at-bat at Fenway. Updike: "Hub Fans Bid Kid Adieu."

seedEntry.title: Ted Williams's final at-bat home run
seedEntry.description: In his final career at-bat at Fenway Park, 42-year-old Ted Williams homered off the Orioles' Jack Fisher into the right-field bullpen — the 521st of his career. He refused a curtain call and stayed in the dugout. John Updike's essay "Hub Fans Bid Kid Adieu" in The New Yorker captured the moment with the line "Gods do not answer letters." Williams declined to play the season's final three games in New York.

entries.name: Clemente's 3,000th hit (1972-09-30)
entries.summary: Sept 30, 1972: Roberto Clemente doubles off Jon Matlack for hit #3,000 — his final regular-season at-bat. He died three months later.

seedEntry.title: Roberto Clemente's 3,000th career hit
seedEntry.description: Pittsburgh's Roberto Clemente doubled off the Mets' Jon Matlack at Three Rivers Stadium for hit #3,000 — the 11th player to reach the milestone. It would be his final regular-season at-bat. Clemente died on December 31, 1972, in a plane crash carrying earthquake aid to Nicaragua. The Hall of Fame waived its 5-year waiting period and inducted him in 1973.

entries.name: Clemens 20-K game (1986-04-29)
entries.summary: April 29, 1986: Roger Clemens strikes out 20 Mariners — first pitcher to ever fan 20 in a 9-inning game.

seedEntry.title: Roger Clemens strikes out 20 batters
seedEntry.description: Boston's 23-year-old Roger Clemens struck out 20 Seattle Mariners at Fenway Park, beating Steve Carlton's and Tom Seaver's 19-K records. He walked none. The Red Sox won 3-1 on a Dwight Evans home run. Clemens did it again on September 18, 1996, also striking out 20.

entries.name: Seaver 19 K + 10 in a row (1970-04-22)
entries.summary: April 22, 1970: Tom Seaver fans 19 Padres including a record 10 in a row to end the game.

seedEntry.title: Tom Seaver strikes out 19 — 10 in a row
seedEntry.description: Mets ace Tom Seaver struck out 19 San Diego Padres at Shea Stadium, including the final 10 batters in order — an MLB record for consecutive strikeouts that still stands. The Mets won 2-1.

entries.name: Ryan's 1st no-hitter (1973-05-15)
entries.summary: May 15, 1973: Nolan Ryan no-hits the Royals — the first of his record seven career no-hitters.

seedEntry.title: Nolan Ryan's first career no-hitter
seedEntry.description: California Angels pitcher Nolan Ryan no-hit the Kansas City Royals 3-0 at Royals Stadium, walking three and striking out 12. It was the first of his MLB-record seven no-hitters; he would throw a second on July 15, 1973 — the only pitcher to throw two in the same year other than Johnny Vander Meer's back-to-back in 1938.

entries.name: Henderson SB record + Ryan no-no #7 (1991-05-01)
entries.summary: May 1, 1991: Rickey Henderson swipes #939 to pass Lou Brock; that same night Nolan Ryan throws his 7th no-hitter at age 44.

seedEntry.title: Henderson breaks Brock's SB record + Ryan's 7th no-hitter
seedEntry.description: Two records on the same day: Rickey Henderson stole his 939th career base in Oakland to pass Lou Brock's all-time record ("today, I am the greatest of all time"); hours later in Texas, 44-year-old Nolan Ryan no-hit the Toronto Blue Jays 3-0 for the seventh no-hitter of his career — a record likely never to be broken.

entries.name: Ryan's 6th no-hitter at 43 (1990-06-11)
entries.summary: June 11, 1990: 43-year-old Nolan Ryan no-hits Oakland — at the time the oldest pitcher to throw a no-hitter.

seedEntry.title: Nolan Ryan's 6th no-hitter — at age 43
seedEntry.description: Texas Rangers pitcher Nolan Ryan, 43, no-hit the Oakland Athletics 5-0 at the Oakland Coliseum. He struck out 14, walked two, and faced an A's lineup including Rickey Henderson, Mark McGwire, Jose Canseco, and Carney Lansford. At the time, Ryan was the oldest pitcher to throw a no-hitter; he broke his own record the next year on May 1, 1991, at age 44.

entries.name: Seaver's 300th win (1985-08-04)
entries.summary: Aug 4, 1985: Tom Seaver, in a White Sox uniform, throws a complete game at Yankee Stadium for win #300.

seedEntry.title: Tom Seaver's 300th career win
seedEntry.description: Chicago White Sox pitcher Tom Seaver beat the New York Yankees 4-1 at Yankee Stadium with a complete-game six-hitter for his 300th career victory. The 40-year-old former Mets ace was pitching against the team that traded him from his original Mets glory; ex-Yankees broadcaster Phil Rizzuto wept on the air during the call.

entries.name: Reggie's 3 HRs Game 6 (1977-10-18)
entries.summary: Oct 18, 1977: Reggie Jackson homers on three consecutive pitches off three Dodgers pitchers in Game 6 of the WS.

seedEntry.title: Reggie Jackson's 3-home-run World Series Game 6
seedEntry.description: Yankees right fielder Reggie Jackson hit three home runs on three consecutive pitches off three different Dodgers pitchers — Burt Hooton, Elias Sosa, and Charlie Hough — in Game 6 of the World Series at Yankee Stadium. New York won 8-4 to clinch their first championship since 1962. The performance cemented Jackson's "Mr. October" nickname.

entries.name: Dodgers first WS title (1955-10-04)
entries.summary: Oct 4, 1955: Johnny Podres shuts out the Yankees 2-0 in Game 7. Brooklyn finally beats New York after seven WS losses.

seedEntry.title: Brooklyn Dodgers' first World Series title
seedEntry.description: Johnny Podres pitched a complete-game shutout to beat the Yankees 2-0 at Yankee Stadium in Game 7 of the World Series — the only championship the Dodgers ever won in Brooklyn. The Daily News headline the next morning: "WHO'S A BUM!" Sandy Amorós's running catch in left field saved a potential game-tying double in the 6th.

entries.name: Morris's 10-inning Game 7 (1991-10-27)
entries.summary: Oct 27, 1991: Jack Morris pitches 10 shutout innings as the Twins beat the Braves 1-0 in Game 7 of one of the greatest WS ever.

seedEntry.title: Jack Morris's 10-inning Game 7 shutout
seedEntry.description: Twins ace Jack Morris pitched 10 scoreless innings against the Braves in Game 7 of the World Series at the Metrodome. Gene Larkin singled home Dan Gladden in the bottom of the 10th to give Minnesota a 1-0 win. The 1991 Series — three games into extra innings, four decided in the final at-bat — is widely regarded as one of the greatest in MLB history. Morris was named World Series MVP.

entries.name: Angels first WS title (2002-10-27)
entries.summary: Oct 27, 2002: Anaheim beats the Giants 4-1 in Game 7 for their first championship in their 42nd season.

seedEntry.title: Anaheim Angels win first World Series
seedEntry.description: The Anaheim Angels beat the San Francisco Giants 4-1 in Game 7 at Edison Field for their first championship in 42 years of franchise history. Garret Anderson's 3rd-inning bases-clearing double broke the game open. Troy Glaus was named World Series MVP after hitting .385 with 3 home runs. The Angels had been one strike from elimination in Game 6.

entries.name: Joe Carter's walk-off WS HR (1993-10-23)
entries.summary: Oct 23, 1993: Joe Carter's 3-run walk-off off Mitch Williams in Game 6 wins the Blue Jays back-to-back titles.

seedEntry.title: Joe Carter's walk-off World Series home run
seedEntry.description: Toronto's Joe Carter hit a 3-run home run off Phillies closer Mitch "Wild Thing" Williams in the bottom of the 9th of Game 6 at SkyDome to win the World Series — only the second walk-off home run to end a World Series, after Bill Mazeroski in 1960. Tom Cheek's call: "Touch 'em all, Joe! You'll never hit a bigger home run in your life!" The Blue Jays were the first repeat champions since the 1977-78 Yankees.

entries.name: Rick Monday saves the flag (1976-04-25)
entries.summary: April 25, 1976: Cubs CF Rick Monday rescues an American flag from two protesters trying to burn it at Dodger Stadium.

seedEntry.title: Rick Monday rescues the flag at Dodger Stadium
seedEntry.description: During the bottom of the 4th of a Cubs-Dodgers game at Dodger Stadium, two protesters ran into left-center field, doused an American flag with lighter fluid, and tried to set it ablaze. Cubs center fielder Rick Monday sprinted over and snatched the flag away before they could light it. The crowd sang "God Bless America." Monday was honored at the 2002 Baseball Hall of Fame ceremony for the act, which is one of the most replayed plays in MLB history.

entries.name: Piazza's post-9/11 HR (2001-09-21)
entries.summary: Sept 21, 2001: Mike Piazza hits an 8th-inning go-ahead 2-run HR in NYC's first major sports event after 9/11.

seedEntry.title: Mike Piazza's post-9/11 home run
seedEntry.description: Ten days after the September 11 attacks, the New York Mets hosted the Atlanta Braves at Shea Stadium in the first major-league sports event in New York after the terror attacks. With the Mets trailing 2-1 in the bottom of the 8th, Mike Piazza hit a go-ahead 2-run home run off Steve Karsay. Mets won 3-2. The moment is widely remembered as a symbolic step in New York's recovery.

entries.name: Babe Ruth's Yankee Stadium farewell (1948-06-13)
entries.summary: June 13, 1948: A frail Babe Ruth, dying of cancer, makes his final appearance at Yankee Stadium for the park's 25th anniversary.

seedEntry.title: Babe Ruth's farewell at Yankee Stadium
seedEntry.description: On the 25th anniversary of Yankee Stadium, an emaciated 53-year-old Babe Ruth — gravely ill with throat cancer — appeared on the field one last time, leaning on a bat for support. The Yankees retired his #3. Nat Fein's photograph "The Babe Bows Out," capturing Ruth from behind, won the 1949 Pulitzer Prize for Photography. Ruth died two months later, on August 16, 1948.

entries.name: AL adopts the DH (1973-01-11)
entries.summary: Jan 11, 1973: AL owners vote 8-4 to adopt the designated hitter rule for a 3-year trial — it has been permanent ever since.

seedEntry.title: American League adopts the designated hitter
seedEntry.description: American League owners voted 8-4 to adopt the designated hitter rule for a three-year trial. Ron Blomberg of the Yankees became the first DH on Opening Day 1973. The rule remained AL-only until 2022, when MLB adopted the universal DH for both leagues.

entries.name: Aaron ties Ruth at 714 (1974-04-04)
entries.summary: April 4, 1974: Hank Aaron homers in his first AB of the season off Jack Billingham to tie Babe Ruth's career HR record.

seedEntry.title: Hank Aaron ties Babe Ruth at 714 home runs
seedEntry.description: On Opening Day in Cincinnati, Atlanta's Hank Aaron homered off Reds pitcher Jack Billingham in his first at-bat of the 1974 season — career HR #714, tying Babe Ruth's all-time record. He passed Ruth four days later on April 8, 1974, with #715 off Al Downing in Atlanta.

entries.name: Bonds passes Ruth at 715 (2006-05-28)
entries.summary: May 28, 2006: Barry Bonds homers off Byung-Hyun Kim for HR #715, passing Babe Ruth into 2nd place all-time.

seedEntry.title: Barry Bonds passes Babe Ruth at 715 career home runs
seedEntry.description: Giants left fielder Barry Bonds hit a 4th-inning home run off Colorado's Byung-Hyun Kim at AT&T Park for career HR #715, moving past Babe Ruth into second place on the all-time home-run list. He would pass Hank Aaron's record of 755 on August 7, 2007, finishing his career with 762.

entries.name: Bonds 71st HR / single-season record (2001-10-05)
entries.summary: Oct 5, 2001: Barry Bonds hits HR #71 off Chan Ho Park, breaking Mark McGwire's 1998 single-season record of 70.

seedEntry.title: Barry Bonds breaks single-season HR record
seedEntry.description: Giants left fielder Barry Bonds hit his 71st home run of the season off Dodgers pitcher Chan Ho Park at Pacific Bell Park in San Francisco, breaking Mark McGwire's 1998 record of 70. Bonds added a 72nd in the same game and finished the season with 73 home runs — still the MLB single-season record.

entries.name: Judge sets rookie HR record (2017-09-25)
entries.summary: Sept 25, 2017: Aaron Judge hits HR #50 to break Mark McGwire's 1987 rookie record of 49.

seedEntry.title: Aaron Judge breaks the MLB rookie home-run record
seedEntry.description: Yankees rookie Aaron Judge hit his 50th home run of the season at Yankee Stadium against the Royals, breaking Mark McGwire's 30-year-old rookie record of 49 home runs from 1987. Judge finished the year with 52 home runs and won AL Rookie of the Year unanimously.

entries.name: Merkle's Boner (1908-09-23)
entries.summary: Sept 23, 1908: Giants rookie Fred Merkle fails to touch second on a winning hit; the call costs NY the pennant.

seedEntry.title: Fred Merkle's Boner
seedEntry.description: With two out in the bottom of the 9th and the Giants and Cubs tied 1-1 at the Polo Grounds, Giants rookie Fred Merkle was on first when Al Bridwell singled in the apparent winning run. Merkle, following common practice of the era, ran toward the dugout without touching second. The Cubs' Johnny Evers retrieved a ball and stepped on second; umpires ruled Merkle out and the game a tie. The Cubs won the makeup game and the NL pennant. The play haunted Merkle for the rest of his career and gave baseball its most enduring "boner" — a synonym for blunder for decades after.
